coredata.dc:title: A logic gate for fluoride anion detection based on carbon dots/gold nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Da-Qian
Liu, Guoliang
Chen, Zhuo
Lu, Hongfei
Gao, Yuting
Fang, Xuebin
coredata.dc:description: A selective logic gate sensor for fluoride anion is first fabricated based on carbon dots/gold nanoparticles hybrid material using 3-mercapto-l,2-propanediol (MP) as the bridge. First, 3-aminophenylboronic acid is chosen as the precursor to fabricate boronic acid decorated carbon dots (b-CDs). Then, b-CDs@MP-gold nanoparticles aggregation is formed by taking advantage of MP as a bridge. As for the occurrence of FRET behavior from b-CDs to gold nanoparticles, the fluorescent signal of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation based sensor quenches remarkably, called as OFF state. However, fluoride anion binds completely with synthesized b-CDs and induces b-CDs to break away from the surface of gold nanoparticles, activating fluorescent signal, named as ON state. As the concentration of fluoride anion increases, the fluorescence signal of the sensor gradually enhances. The fluorescent response for fluoride anion detection is in the linear range from 9 μM to 117 μM with the detection limit of 1.5 μM. Moreover, the b-CDs@MP-Au NPs based sensor is operated as a logic gate at the molecular level. The experimental results indicate that our present approach can be adapted for establishing the chemical logic gate for the fluoride anion. Furthermore, our proposed strategy holds great potential application in environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Off-on fluorescent sensor
Carbon dots
FRET
Logic device
Fluoride anion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the pre-prepared b-CDs and Au NPs
Characterization of the b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation
Fluorescent off-on detection of fluoride anion by using b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation based sensor
Construction of label-free logic gate device based on activated fluorescent sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of synthesized b-CDs upon the addition of different concentration of MP-Au NPs complex. Conditions: 1, b-CDs; 2–5: 1 + MP-Au NPs (nM): 0.33, 0.67, 1, and 1.33.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs assembly based sensor in the absence and presence of different amounts of fluoride anion. Conditions: 1, b-CDs@MP-Au NPs based sensor; 2–14: 1 + F− anion (μM): 9, 18, 27, 36, 45, 54, 63, 72, 81, 90, 99, 108, and 117. The concentration of MP-Au NPs is 1.33 nM. (B) The linear correlation between the increment of fluorescent intensity (F-F0) and the concentrations of fluoride anion.

Fluorescence response of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs assembly based sensor to F− anion or various potential interfering substances. The metal ions concentrations of K+, Na+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Fe3+ are 30 μM. The concentrations of BSA, Sucrose, Fructose, Lactose, and Maltose are 40 μM. The anions concentrations of HCO3 2−, HPO4 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, Br−, I−, and CH3COO− are 50 μM. The concentrations of F− and glucose are 36 μM.

Operation of the four-input logic gate system of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs assembly based sensor. (A) Schematic illustration of four-input logic gate by b-CDs@MP-Au NPs assembly based sensor. (B) Truth table for the four-put logic gate.

(A) Schematic illustration of the sensing mechanism of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots/gold nanoparticles for fluoride ion determination. (B) Schematic representation of a logic gate obtained from b-CDs@MP-AuNPs aggregation in the absence and presence of F− anion. (C) Truth table for the four-put logic gate.
Compared the results of F− determination in tap water and river water sample by using the present approach with the ICP-AES method (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average values are from three independent measurements.

head.ce:title: A logic gate for fluoride anion detection based on carbon dots/gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A selective logic gate sensor for fluoride anion is first fabricated based on carbon dots/gold nanoparticles hybrid material using 3-mercapto-l,2-propanediol (MP) as the bridge. First, 3-aminophenylboronic acid is chosen as the precursor to fabricate boronic acid decorated carbon dots (b-CDs). Then, b-CDs@MP-gold nanoparticles aggregation is formed by taking advantage of MP as a bridge. As for the occurrence of FRET behavior from b-CDs to gold nanoparticles, the fluorescent signal of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation based sensor quenches remarkably, called as OFF state. However, fluoride anion binds completely with synthesized b-CDs and induces b-CDs to break away from the surface of gold nanoparticles, activating fluorescent signal, named as ON state. As the concentration of fluoride anion increases, the fluorescence signal of the sensor gradually enhances. The fluorescent response for fluoride anion detection is in the linear range from 9 μM to 117 μM with the detection limit of 1.5 μM. Moreover, the b-CDs@MP-Au NPs based sensor is operated as a logic gate at the molecular level. The experimental results indicate that our present approach can be adapted for establishing the chemical logic gate for the fluoride anion. Furthermore, our proposed strategy holds great potential application in environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluoride anion, belonging to the heavy metal countercations, is one of the major pollutants of water. The fluoride polluted water is rapidly increasing due to the industrial growth and human activities [1]. The proper water fluoridation is beneficial for preventing from dental caries [2]. However, the excessive intake of fluoride causes human poisoning attributing to its slow excretion from human body [3]. Moreover, the excess ingestion also results in some diseases including tooth and skeletal fluorosis, osteoporosis, kidney failure and nephrolithiasis, lesions of thyroid, liver and other organs, and the inhibition of neurotransmitter biosynthesis in fetuses [4–6]. The World Health Organization (WHO) and Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have set the maximum concentration limit of fluoride anion at 1.5 mg/L (78.9 μmol/L) in drinking water [7,8]. Traditional methods for the fluoride anion detection possess inevitable defects such as requiring expensive reagents, cumbersome sample preparation, sophisticated instrumentation, lower sensitivity or selectivity, or narrow linear range [9–14]. Therefore, developing economical, facile, high selectivity and sensitivity detection approach of fluoride anion is of great significance.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) are attracting a great deal of attention due to good water solubility, unique optical property, low toxicity, and high biocompatibility [15,16]. So far, CDs have been fabricated with different carbon source and used to detect several kinds of toxins such as copper ions [17] and fluoride anions [18–20]. However, cumbersome preparation process and unclear sensing mechanism limit these methods’ further application. There is a need to develop a simple material based approach with a suitable working mechanism.

Herein, a new FRET sensor for fluoride anion detection based on b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation by using MP as a bridge is first developed. By employing b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation as the building block and using the fluorescence signal as the output, a logic device for fluoride anion is also constructed (Scheme 1 ). The 3-aminophenylboronic acid decorated carbon dots (b-CDs) are fabricated by one-step synthesis-modification integration method, which are selected as energy donor meanwhile Au NPs are chosen as energy acceptor. By using MP as the bridge, the b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation forms and FRET behavior from b-CDs to Au NPs occurs, leading to quenched fluorescence signal of the sensor (Scheme 1A). Interestingly, fluoride anion competitively binds with the boron group on the surface of b-CDs and disturbs the b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation, resulting in enhanced fluorescence signal of the sensor. Based on these off-on fluorescence signal changes, a new strategy for fluoride anion detection is developed in a single operation run by using b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation based fluorescent sensor (Scheme 1B and C). Moreover, other interfering substances do not generate positive signal. Furthermore, the sensing efficiency of the sensor is also applied to detect fluoride anion in real samples with satisficed results.

3-Aminophenylboronic acid, sodium citrate and 3-mercapto-1,2-propanediol are purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation (China). Sodium fluoride (NaF), sodium bromide (NaBr) and sodium iodide (NaI) are purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (China). Tetrachloroauric (III) acid hydrate and other reagents are all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All reagents used are of analytical grade without further purification or the best grade commercially available. Millipore Milli-Q ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ. cm) is used in all experiments. The phosphoric acid buffer solution (PBS) is used to control the acidity of the solution, which is made up of different proportions of sodium dihydrogen phosphate and disodium hydrogen phosphate.

The fluorescence spectra are recorded with a fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco FP-6500, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra are carried out by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 245, Japan). Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra are obtained by using a FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet-6700, Thermo Ltd., USA) within a range of 400–4000 cm−1. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are recorded on a field emission transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV (JEM-2100F, JEOL, Ltd., Japan). All pH measurements are made with an acidity meter (DELTA 320, Mettler-Toledo Instruments Co. Ltd., China).

3-aminophenylboronic acid decorated carbon dots (b-CDs) are fabricated by one-step synthesis-modification integration method. Firstly, 0.2 g 3-aminophenylboronic acid is dissolved in distilled water and pH value of the system is adjusted to pH 9. Then, the mixture is transferred into polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and reacts for 9 h at 160 °C. After the reaction, the solution is naturally cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the impurities with large particles. Moreover, the supernatants are further purified through a dialysis membrane tubing (1000 Da, molecular weight cut-off, Viskase). The obtained b-CDs solution is stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for further study.

Gold nanoparticles (16 nm) are prepared by the citrate reduction of HAuCl4 [21]. Briefly, after boiling 100 mL HAuCl4 solution (0.01%), 3.5 mL trisodium citrate solution (1.0%) is rapidly introduced under vigorous stirring condition. The solution color changes to deep red in a few seconds and the reduction of trisodium citrate to HAuCl4 completes after further 15 min boiling. The mixture is naturally cooled to room temperature and diluted to 100 mL with the ultrapure water.

By mixing 2 μL of 3-mercapto-1,2-propanediol (MP) with 80 mL of gold nanoparticles and reacting for 10 h in the dark, the MP modified Au NPs (MP-Au NPs) are pre-prepared. After that, b-CDs@MP-Au NPs nanocomposites are fabricated by mixing 4 mL prepared b-CDs solution with 80 mL of MP-Au NPs for 1 h.

First, 1 mL of the prepared b-CDs@MP-Au NPs based sensor solution is placed in 5 mL calibrated tubes. Then, a series of different concentrations of fluoride anion solutions are added into the sensor solution. After that, the mixtures are diluted with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) to a final volume of 3 mL. The samples are removed from the calibrated tubes and placed in a cuvette for fluorescence spectra measurements. The fluorescent spectrum is recorded in the 300–550 nm emission wavelength range at an excitation wavelength of 293 nm [22,23]. The emission intensity is measured at the maximum wavelength with slit width at 5 nm for the excitation and emission. Based on the spectrum, the fluorescent intensity located at 293 nm is recorded. The change value of fluorescence intensity of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs based sensor increases by the addition of fluoride anion is represented as ΔF = F − F0 , where F0 and F are fluorescent intensities of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs based sensor in the absence and presence of the fluoride anion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the pre-prepared b-CDs and Au NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The boronic acid functionalized carbon dots (b-CDs) are synthesized by one-pot synthesis-modification integration strategy with 3-aminophenylboronic acid as the precursor (Scheme 1A). The fluorescence spectra of fabricated b-CDs are shown in Fig. S1A (Please see the Supplementary Material). The maximum emission peak is located at 406 nm while the excitation wavelength is set up at 293 nm. Besides, the surface structure of the as-prepared b-CDs is studied by FT-IR spectrum while the 3-aminophenylboronic acid is selected as the control (Fig. S1B). The FT-IR spectrum of synthesized b-CDs shows distinct absorption bands located at 1028 and 1350 cm−1, attributing to B-O–H deformation vibration and B-O stretching vibration, respectively [24,25]. The experimental results reveal that boronic acid groups are successfully transferred from 3-aminophenylboronic acid to the surface of prepared b-CDs. Moreover, the Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) image of fabricated b-CDs is displayed in Fig. S1C, demonstrating that the as-prepared b-CDs are well-dispersed with 3 nm average diameter. These experimental assays suggest the successful synthesis of b-CDs. In addition, the synthesized gold nanoparticles are also characterized by typical TEM image and the result is displayed in Fig. S1D. The image reveals that the Au NPs possess uniform morphology with 16 nm size, revealing successful preparation of Au NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: As for possessing sulfhydryl group and hydroxyl group, 3-mercapto-l,2-propanediol (MP) is selected as a bridge for constructing fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system. The esterification between carboxyl group on the surface of b-CDs and hydroxyl group of MP is in favor for the fabricated b-CDs@MP while the S-Au bond between MP and Au NPs is responsible for the constructed MP-Au NPs complex, resulting in the formation of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation.

UV–vis spectrum of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation is then investigated. As displayed in Fig. S2 (Please see the Supplementary Material), the UV–vis spectrum of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation (the green line) exhibits distinct absorption bands at 285 nm and 520 nm, attributing to fabricated b-CDs and synthesized Au NPs, respectively, compared with those of synthesized b-CDs (the blue line) or pre-prepared Au NPs only (the red line). These experimental phenomena suggest that b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation is successfully formed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent off-on detection of fluoride anion by using b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of fabricated b-CDs in the absence and presence of MP-Au NPs complex are displayed in Fig. 1 . The experimental results indicate that the synthesized b-CDs possess strong fluorescence signal and begin to decrease significantly after the addition of MP-Au NPs. It is due to the formation of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation by using MP as the bridge and then the occurrence of FRET behavior from b-CDs to Au NPs. Interestingly, with the increase of MP-Au NPs concentration, the fluorescence intensity of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation based sensor located at the maximum peak gradually decreases (Fig. 1). After the addition of 1.33 nM MP-Au NPs complex, the fluorescent signal of the sensor nearly quenches due to FRET mechanism, called as OFF state.

In order to investigate the mechanism of the b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation based sensor, the fluorescent responses for different concentrations of F− anion are detected (Fig. 2 ). The b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation displays a quenched fluorescent signal (curve 1), which can be easily explained by the fact that the occurrence of FRET from b-CDs to Au NPs [26]. When F− anions bind to the boron group of prepared b-CDs, the fluorescent signal increases remarkably owing to the disaggregation behavior of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation that prevents the energy transfer process [27]. Once the F− anion binds with b-CDs, it turns up significant enhancement of the emission intensity at maximum peak. When the introduction of 117 μM F−, the fluorescent intensity of the sensor increases to approximately 97.4% (the fluorescent recovery rate) because of the famous Lewis acid-base reaction based on the affinity of boron toward the fluoride anion [28] and makes b-CDs separate from the MP-Au NPs. The more amount of b-CDs-F− complex will lead to the more disaggregation of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation and then the more b-CDs-F− complex will break away from the surface of the sensor. Consequently, the concentration of F− anions can be quantified by the increased fluorescent intensity produced by the removed b-CDs-F− complex. The quenched fluorescent intensity of b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation based sensor is significantly activated upon the addition of F− anion solution, called as ON state.

The emission spectra of the fabricated sensor clearly illustrate the rapid response in different concentrations of F−. As can be seen in Fig. 2A, the fluorescence intensity gradually increases with increased concentration of F−. Furthermore, a linear relationship between the change of fluorescent intensity (F-F0) and the concentration of F− anion is obtained, in which F0 and F represents the fluorescent intensity of the developed sensor in the absence and presence of fluoride anion, respectively. The good linear range is obtained from to 9 μM to 117 μM with the limit of detection 1.5 mΜ (Fig. 2B). The limit of detection is detected by 3S0/S, where 3 is the factor at the 99% confidence level, S0 is the standard deviation of the 10 blank determinations, and S is the sensitivity of the calibration graph. Compared with those of reported electrochemical sensors [12,29], colorimetric sensor [30], and fluorescent sensors [31–34], our detection limit for fluoride anion detection is much lower or comparable.

To investigate the selectivity of our proposed approach, the influence of various kinds of potential interfering substances including common anions such as Cl−, Br−, I−, HCO3 2−, HPO4 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, and CH3COO−, metal cations consisting of K+, Na+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Fe3+, BSA, Sucrose, Fructose, Lactose, Maltose, and Glucose on the fluorescent response of the fabricated sensor is estimated. As displayed in Fig. 3 , the experimental results reveal that most substances have very little effects (fluorescent quenching or enhancement) on the b-CDs fluorescence. However, the glucose has a certain degree of disturbance on the detecting system owing to the specific reaction between glucose and boronic acid group [27,28]. Glucose impurity needs to be removed before the sample determination. Therefore, the proposed method can be used for selective and quantitative analysis of fluoride anion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of label-free logic gate device based on activated fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: After the completion of the sensing assays, the performance of a logic gate function is carried out based on the interaction between the Au NPs and MP and fluoride anion and synthesized b-CDs. To execute the logic gate, the introduction of Au NPs, MP, fluoride anion and other potential interfering substances is selected as the four inputs, whereas the variation of the fluorescent signal is applied as an output. The absence and presence of these inputs in terms of prepared b-CDs are defined as the 0 and 1 states, respectively. The outputs define as 1 or 0 which corresponds to strong and weak fluorescent response, respectively. The logic gate is chosen to study the variation in the fluorescence signal of the b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation with fluoride anion as an input signal. The fluoride anion activates the fluorescent intensity of the b-CDs@MP-Au NPs based sensor, leading to an output signal of 1. A schematic representation of the logic gate is given in Fig. 4 A. These experimental assays correlate with the function of the AND logic gate. Moreover, a logic operation can be realized by controlling the concentration of fluoride anion to generate a truth table (Fig. 4B).

In order to investigate the reliability of our proposed assay in real samples, the approach of sensing fluoride anions in tap water and river water samples is also carried out. The amount of the analyte is determined by the standard addition method. Meanwhile, traditional ICP-AES method is also performed by inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer (Perkin Elmer 4300DV, USA). As shown in Table 1 , good consistency between the fluoride anion detection amounts obtained by both methods is observed, indicating the excellent behavior of our proposed approach in environmental monitoring.

In summary, a label-free approach for environmental pollutant fluoride anions is developed based on FRET mechanism by using b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation. It is demonstrated that energy acceptor Au NPs assemble on the surface of boronic acid decorated carbon dots which is selected as energy donor by using MP as the bridge, turning-off the fluorescent signal of the sensor owing to the FRET behavior from the donor to the acceptor. However, fluoride anion competitively binds with b-CDs from b-CDs@MP-Au NPs aggregation and disturbed the FREF process, activating the quenched fluorescent signal of the sensor. In view of these impressive results, the molecular AND logic gate by using metal ions or anions as the chemical inputs for the practical application is developed. Moreover, the present approach is applied to detect fluoride anion in real samples and the results are compared with the traditional ICP-AES method. Furthermore, the new strategy of the construction of fluorescent based logic gate device maybe expands the scope of the potential applications of carbon-based nanomaterials in label-free optical bioassays, molecular-level biochemical sensing, molecular electronic and nanodevices.

Da-Qian Feng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Guoliang Liu: Supervision, Resources, Software, Writing - review & editing. Zhuo Chen: Investigation, Data curation. Hongfei Lu: Investigation. Yuting Gao: Validation. Xuebin Fang: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China (21501146), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20150424 and BK20140464), the Opening Foundation of the Key Laboratory for Preparation and Application of Ordered Structural Materials of Guangdong Province (KLPAOSM201507), the Opening Project of Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biochemistry and Biotechnology of Marine Wetland (K2016-18), and this work was also sponsored by Qinglan Project of Jiangsu Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.104977.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluoride in the environment: source, distribution, and defluoridation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Vithanage, P. Bhattacharya, Fluoride in the environment: source, distribution, and defluoridation, Environ. Chem. Lett. 13 (2015) 131–147.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluoride varishes for preventing dental caries in children and adolescents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.C.C. Marinho, H.V. Worthington, T. Walsh, J.E. Clarkson, Fluoride varishes for preventing dental caries in children and adolescents, Cochrane Database Syst. Rev. 7 (2013) CD002279.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent metal organic frameworks–based chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer platform for turn–on detection of fluoride ion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Sun, X.T. Xu, Y.X. Zhao, H.N. Tan, Y.H. Li, J.X. Du, Luminescent metal organic frameworks–based chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer platform for turn–on detection of fluoride ion, Talanta 209 (2020) 120582.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluoride in drinking water and defluoridation of water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Jagtap, M.K. Yenkie, N. Labhsetwar, S. Rayalu, Fluoride in drinking water and defluoridation of water, Chem. Rev. 112 (2012) 2454–2466.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective colorimetric/fluorometric dual-channel fluoride ion probe, and its capability of differentiating cancer cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zheng, W. Zhu, D. Liu, H. Ai, Y. Huang and Z. Lu, Highly selective colorimetric/fluorometric dual-channel fluoride ion probe, and its capability of differentiating cancer cells, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 6 (2014) 7996–8000.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in chromo-fluorogenic probes for fluoride detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.L. Han, J. Zhang, M.Q. Gao, H.P. Hao, X.W. Xu, Recent advances in chromo-fluorogenic probes for fluoride detection, Dyes Pigments 162 (2019) 412–439.
J. Fawell, K. Bailey, J. Chilton, E. Dahi, L. Fewtrell, Y. Magara, Fluoride in drinking water, first ed., International Water Association, London, 2006.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: World Health Organization, Guidelines for drinking water quality recommendation, fourth ed., (2011), pp. 285–287.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Azulene–boronate esters: colorimetric indicators for fluoride in drinking water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.M. López-Alled, A.S. Sanchez-Fernandez, K.J. Edler, A.C. Sedgwick, S.D. Bull, C.L. McMullin, G. Kociok-Köhn, T.D. James, J. Wenk, S.E. Lewis, Azulene–boronate esters: colorimetric indicators for fluoride in drinking water, Chem. Commun. 53 (2017) 12580−12583.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel and convenient near-infrared fluorescence “turn off–on” nanosensor for detection of glucose and fluoride anions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.P. Liu, L.L. Laiu, M.H. Sun, X.G. Su, A novel and convenient near-infrared fluorescence “turn off–on” nanosensor for detection of glucose and fluoride anions, Biosens. Bioelectron. 53 (2015) 145−151.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct detection of fluoride ions in aquatic samples by surface–enhanced Raman scattering

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, M. Zhang, Y. Wang, X. Wang, H. Ma, P. Li, W. Song, X.X. Han, B. Zhao, Direct detection of fluoride ions in aquatic samples by surface–enhanced Raman scattering, Talanta 178 (2018) 9–14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of a selective electrochemical sensing solid–liquid interface for the selective detection of fluoride ion in water with bis(indolyl)methane–functionalized multi–walled carbon nanotubes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ni, H. Liu, J. Xu, Y. Yue, S. Shao, Construction of a selective electrochemical sensing solid–liquid interface for the selective detection of fluoride ion in water with bis(indolyl)methane–functionalized multi–walled carbon nanotubes, New J. Chem. 41 (2017) 14246–14252.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of trace fluoride in serum and urine by online membrance–based distillation coupled with ion chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.Y. Lou, D.D. Guo, N.N. Wang, S.C. Wu, P.M. Zhang, Y. Zhu, Detection of trace fluoride in serum and urine by online membrance–based distillation coupled with ion chromatography, J. Chromatogr. A 1500 (2017) 145–152.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green, water-dispersible photoluminescent On–Off–On probe for selective detection of fluoride ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Singhal, B.G. Vats, S.K. Jha, S. Neogy, Green, water-dispersible photoluminescent On–Off–On probe for selective detection of fluoride ions, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 9 (2017) 20536–20544.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.C. Sun, Y. Lei, Fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications, TrAC Trend. Anal. Chem. 89 (2017) 163−180.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.Q. Ding, A.W. Zhu, Y. Tian, Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging, Acc. Chem. Res. 47 (2014) 20−30.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Q. Dong, R.X. Wang, G.L. Li, C.Q. Chen, Y.W. Chi, G.N. Chen, Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions, Anal. Chem. 84 (2012) 6220–6224.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of Fe3O4@SiO2@carbon quantum dot based nanostructure for fluorescence sensing, magnetic separation, and live cell imaging of fluoride ion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mohapatra, S. Sahu, S. Nayak, S.K. Ghosh, Design of Fe3O4@SiO2@carbon quantum dot based nanostructure for fluorescence sensing, magnetic separation, and live cell imaging of fluoride ion, Langmuir 31 (2015) 8111–8120.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dopamine integrated B, N, S doped CQD nanoprobe for rapid and selective detection of fluoride ion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mohapatra, R.K. Das, Dopamine integrated B, N, S doped CQD nanoprobe for rapid and selective detection of fluoride ion, Anal. Chim. Acta 1058 (2019) 146–154.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Implementation of a logic gate by chemically induced nitrogen and oxygen rich C-dots for the selective detection of fluoride ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kainth, A. Mehta, A. Mishra, S. Basu, Implementation of a logic gate by chemically induced nitrogen and oxygen rich C-dots for the selective detection of fluoride ions, New J. Chem. 42 (2018) 12162–12171.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective and sensitive on-off sensor for silver ions and cysteine by light scattering technique of DNA-functionalized gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.-Q. Feng, G.L. Liu, W.J. Zheng, J. Liu, T.F. Chen, D. Li, A highly selective and sensitive on-off sensor for silver ions and cysteine by light scattering technique of DNA-functionalized gold nanoparticles, Chem. Commun. 47 (2011) 8557–8559.
